title: Contributing to postlink
text: Files grouped four main categories based role package architecture: Foundation Utilities: 00_utils.R: Shared internal helper functions used across various methods. postlink-package.R: core package-level documentation namespace directives. stanmodels.R: interface pre-compiled Stan/C++ models used Bayesian adjustments. Phase 1: Adjustment Constructors (01_ Prefix): files contain user-facing constructor functions define linked data specify adjustment method. 01_adjELE.R, 01_adjMixture.R, 01_adjMixBayes.R: core constructor functions. 01_adjELE_methods.R, etc.: S3 methods (like print, summary) specifically adjustment objects. Phase 2: Wrapper Models (02_ Prefix): files contain user-facing modeling functions parse formulas dispatch appropriate internal routine based provided adjustment object. 02_plglm.R, 02_plcoxph.R, 02_plctable.R, 02_plsurvreg.R Internal Routines Adjustment Methods: internal computational routines completely decoupled wrappers. named using [method]_[model] syntax. ELE: ele_glm.R, ele_coxph.R, associated *_methods.R files. Mixture: mixture_glm.R, mixture_coxph.R, mixture_ctable.R, mixture_helpers.R, etc. Bayesian Mixture: mixbayes_glm.R, mixbayes_survreg.R, mixbayes_helpers.R, etc. Datasets: lifem.R, brfss.R: Roxygen documentation included demo datasets.

title: Post-Linkage Data Analysis
text: postlink package dedicated providing unified suite statistical tools designed rigorously account record linkage errors post-linkage data analysis. Record linkage often error-prone, particularly identifiers used matching records noisy non-unique. Mismatches (false matches) act contaminant linked data, typically leading attenuated estimates downstream analysis. postlink R package currently supports three statistical frameworks account potential mismatch errors downstream regression modeling: Weighting: Solves adjusted estimating equations using block-specific proportions correct links. assumes Exchangeable Linkage Error (ELE) model (Chambers, 2009; Chambers et al., 2023; Vo et al., 2024). Mixture Modeling: Uses Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm infers latent correct match status using two-component mixture model (Slawski et al., 2025). Bayesian Mixture Modeling: Performs posterior inference via data augmentation, alternating imputing latent match status updating model parameters propagate linkage uncertainty (Gutman et al., 2016). postlink package currently focuses methods secondary analysis, individual files linked accessible. primary analysis setting, individual files accessible, methods perform record linkage analysis jointly direct propagation uncertainty suitable. long-term goal postlink extend support wide array linkage post-linkage analysis scenarios.

title: Post-Linkage Data Analysis
text: brief example illustrating typical workflow using postlink. analyze relationship age death year birth linked using historical records LIFE-M project. linked dataset contains mix hand-linked records (assumed correct) purely machine-linked records subject approximate 5% mismatch rate. Instead fitting standard glm model ignoring mismatch errors, use postlink adjust potential mismatches using entire linked dataset.
code: library(postlink)  # Load the LIFE-M demo dataset data(lifem)  # Phase 1: Adjustment Specification # We model the correct match indicator via logistic regression using  # name commonness scores (commf, comml) and a 5% expected mismatch rate. adj_object <- adjMixture(   linked.data = lifem,   m.formula = ~ commf + comml,   m.rate = 0.05,   safe.matches = hndlnk )  # Check specified adjustment print(adj_object)  # Phase 2: Estimation & Inference # Fit a Gaussian regression model utilizing a cubic polynomial for year of birth. fit <- plglm(   age_at_death ~ poly(unit_yob, 3, raw = TRUE),   family = "gaussian",   adjustment = adj_object )  # View model results summary(fit) confint(fit)

title: Confidence Intervals for coxphELE Objects — confint.coxphELE
code: library(survival) set.seed(104) n <- 200  # 1. Simulate covariates age_centered <- rnorm(n, 0, 5) treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # 2. Simulate true survival times true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.05 * age_centered - 0.6 * treatment)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.2) time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) status <- as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time)  # 3. Induce 15% Exchangeable Linkage Error (ELE) mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.15 * n)) linked_age <- age_centered linked_trt <- treatment  # False links drawn uniformly from the target population false_link_idx <- sample(1:n, size = length(mis_idx), replace = TRUE) linked_age[mis_idx] <- age_centered[false_link_idx] linked_trt[mis_idx] <- treatment[false_link_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(time = time, status = status,                           age = linked_age, treatment = linked_trt)  # 4. Fit the adjusted Cox PH model adj <- adjELE(linked.data = linked_data, m.rate = 0.15) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + treatment, adjustment = adj)  # 5. Compute confidence intervals confint(fit) # 95% CI for all coefficients #>                2.5 %     97.5 % #> age       -0.0853233 0.06064571 #> treatment -0.6688953 0.89980482 confint(fit, parm = "treatment", level = 0.90) # 90% CI for a specific parameter #>                  5 %      95 % #> treatment -0.5427925 0.7737021

code: library(survival) set.seed(201)  # Simulate survival data (N = 200) n <- 200 x1 <- rnorm(n) x2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.5 * x1 - 0.5 * x2)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)  # Simulate auxiliary match scores and linkage errors # Lower match scores correspond to a higher probability of mismatch match_score <- runif(n, 0.5, 1.0) is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = 1 - match_score)  # Induce linkage errors by shuffling covariates of mismatched records linked_x1 <- x1 linked_x2 <- x2 mis_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1) shuffled_idx <- sample(mis_idx) linked_x1[mis_idx] <- x1[shuffled_idx] linked_x2[mis_idx] <- x2[shuffled_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(   time = pmin(true_time, cens_time),   status = as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time),   x1 = linked_x1, x2 = linked_x2,   match_score = match_score )  # Fit the Cox PH Mixture Model (Slawski et al., 2023) adj <- adjMixture(linked.data = linked_data, m.formula = ~ match_score) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ x1 + x2, adjustment = adj,                control = list(max.iter = 15))  # Extract Confidence Intervals confint(fit) #>                  2.5 %     97.5 % #> x1           0.6710011  1.6021040 #> x2          -2.1429623 -0.4823973 #> (Intercept)         NA         NA #> match_score         NA         NA confint(fit, parm = "treatment", level = 0.90) #>           5 % 95 % #> treatment  NA   NA

title: Fit a CoxPH Model Assuming Exchangeable Linkage Errors — coxphELE
code: set.seed(101) n <- 250  # 1. Simulate true data age <- rnorm(n, 60, 8) treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # Simulate true hazard # Older age and lack of treatment increase hazard true_hazard <- exp(0.05 * (age - 60) - 0.5 * treatment) true_time <- rexp(n, rate = true_hazard) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.1)  obs_time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) status <- as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time) # 1 = event, 0 = censored  # 2. Induce 15% exchangeable linkage errors mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.15 * n)) linked_age <- age linked_treatment <- treatment false_link_idx <- sample(1:n, size = length(mis_idx), replace = TRUE) linked_age[mis_idx] <- age[false_link_idx] linked_treatment[mis_idx] <- treatment[false_link_idx]  # 3. Prepare matrices for the internal routine cens_ele <- 1 - status X_mat <- cbind(age = linked_age, treatment = linked_treatment)  # 4. Fit the model directly # cens = 1 for censored, 0 for event fit_ele <- coxphELE(x = X_mat, y = obs_time, cens = cens_ele, m.rate = 0.15) print(fit_ele$coefficients) #>          age    treatment  #>  0.017052210 -0.001040659

title: CoxPH with Mixture-Based Linkage Error Adjustment — coxphMixture
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(survival) set.seed(123) n <- 200 # Generate covariates x_cov <- seq(-3, 3, length = n) d_cov <- rep(0:1, each = n/2) X <- cbind(d_cov, x_cov, x_cov * d_cov)  # True parameters b <- c(-1.5, 1, 0.5) sigma <- 0.25 mu <- X %*% b y <- exp(drop(mu)) * rweibull(n, shape = 1/sigma)  # Censoring cens <- (y >= 1.5) y[cens] <- 1.5  # Generate mismatch errors ps <- rbeta(n, 4.5, 0.5) logit_ps <- log(ps / (1 - ps)) mp <- cbind(1, logit_ps) gamma_true <- c(-0.5, 1) m <- 1 - rbinom(n, prob = plogis(mp %*% gamma_true), size = 1) yperm <- y shuffled_ix <- sample(which(m == 1)) yperm[shuffled_ix] <- yperm[sample(shuffled_ix)]  # Fit model fit <- coxphMixture(x = X, y = yperm, cens = as.numeric(cens),                     z = matrix(logit_ps, ncol = 1),                     control = list(max.iter = 50))  print(fit) summary(fit) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate Synthetic Data set.seed(1234) K <- 3; L <- 4 n <- 1000 # Define true probabilities for a KxL table cellprobs <- c(0.18, 0.05, 0.03, 0.04, 0.02, 0.14,                0.02, 0.02, 0.10, 0.21, 0.15, 0.04) matrix_probs <- matrix(cellprobs, nrow = K, ncol = L)  # Generate multinomial counts dat <- stats::rmultinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = cellprobs) obs_idx <- apply(dat, 2, function(x) which(x == 1)) X <- ceiling(obs_idx / L) # Row indices Y <- (obs_idx %% L); Y[Y == 0] <- L # Col indices  # Introduce Linkage Error (Mismatches) alpha <- 0.20 # 20% mismatch rate n_mismatch <- round(n * alpha) Y_perm <- Y # Shuffle the first n_mismatch Y values to break dependence Y_perm[1:n_mismatch] <- sample(Y[1:n_mismatch])  # Create Observed Table (with error) tab_obs <- table(X, Y_perm)  # Apply Adjustment Method fit <- ctableMixture(tab = tab_obs, m.rate = alpha)  # Inspect Results print(fit$converged) # Compare estimated Correct Counts vs True Counts (approx) print(round(fit$ftable)) print(round(table(X, Y))) # True table without errors } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(301) n <- 150 trt <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # Simulate Weibull AFT data true_time <- rweibull(n, shape = 1.5, scale = exp(1 + 0.8 * trt)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.1) true_obs_time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) true_status <- as.integer(true_time <= cens_time)  # Induce linkage mismatch errors in approximately 20% of records is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.2) obs_time <- true_obs_time obs_status <- true_status mismatch_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1)  shuffled <- sample(mismatch_idx) obs_time[mismatch_idx] <- obs_time[shuffled] obs_status[mismatch_idx] <- obs_status[shuffled]  linked_df <- data.frame(time = obs_time, status = obs_status, trt = trt) adj <- adjMixBayes(linked.data = linked_df)  fit <- plsurvreg(   survival::Surv(time, status) ~ trt,   dist = "weibull",   adjustment = adj,   control = list(iterations = 200, burnin.iterations = 100, seed = 123) )  pooled_obj <- mi_with(   object = fit,   data = linked_df,   formula = survival::Surv(time, status) ~ trt )  print(pooled_obj) } # }

what: postlink: Post-Linkage Data Analysis Under Record Linkage Errors — postlink-package
title: postlink: Post-Linkage Data Analysis Under Record Linkage Errors — postlink-package
text: postlink package provides unified suite statistical tools designed rigorously account record linkage errors downstream modeling. Record linkage often error-prone. datasets merged using noisy non-unique identifiers, mismatches (false links) inadvertently introduced. Ignoring errors acts contaminant regression analysis, typically leading significantly attenuated estimates biased statistical inference. postlink equips researchers methodologies propagate linkage uncertainty models, specifically accommodating "secondary analysis" workflows direct access primary, unlinked files restricted.

title: postlink: Post-Linkage Data Analysis Under Record Linkage Errors — postlink-package
text: Two-Phase Workflow package built modular, object-oriented S3 architecture decouples specification linkage error substantive statistical modeling. provides familiar, standard formula-based modeling interface. Phase 1: Adjustment Specification analyst encapsulates linked data chosen error-adjustment methodology using one adj* constructor functions. functions validate data return structured adjustment object: adjELE(): Specifies Exchangeable Linkage Error (ELE) model, corrects bias using known audited mismatch rates. adjMixture(): Specifies frequentist mixture model approach treats match status latent variable, estimating error rates directly data (e.g., using match scores) via EM algorithm. adjMixBayes(): Specifies Bayesian mixture model approach, enabling parameter estimation multiple imputation latent match statuses using Stan. Phase 2: Estimation & Inference adjustment object subsequently passed standard modeling wrapper, integrating error correction familiar R modeling syntax: plglm(): Generalized Linear Models (linear, logistic, Poisson, Gamma). plcoxph(): Cox Proportional Hazards models. plctable(): Contingency table analyses. plsurvreg(): Parametric survival models. note, estimation inference supported type adjustment object vary. Refer adj* documentation models currently supported. Post-estimation tools function expected standard R workflows (e.g., summary(), predict(), vcov(), confint()). methods specially  derived account additional steps introduced linkage error adjustment. Note: two-phase workflow recommended standard analyses, package's architecture intentionally isolates core logic method independent internal routines. Advanced users, developers, researchers running large-scale simulations can bypass wrapper functions formula-parsing overhead entirely. supplying pre-computed design matrices response vectors, underlying computational functions can directly used preferred (e.g., coxphELE(), glmMixture(), survregMixBayes()).

code: library(survival) set.seed(105)  # Simulate linked data subject to mismatch error n <- 200 x1 <- rnorm(n) x2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.7) true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.5 * x1 - 0.5 * x2)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)  # Induce 15% linkage error mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = 0.15 * n) x1[mis_idx] <- x1[sample(mis_idx)] x2[mis_idx] <- x2[sample(mis_idx)]  # Linked data linked_data <- data.frame(   time = pmin(true_time, cens_time),   status = as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time),   x1 = x1, x2 = x2 )  # Fit the adjusted Cox PH model adj <- adjELE(linked.data = linked_data, m.rate = 0.15) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ x1 + x2, adjustment = adj)  # 1. Extract linear predictors for the original data lp_train <- predict(fit, type = "lp") head(lp_train) #> [1] -0.007068736 -0.042136465 -0.026928050 -0.043077001 -0.026705796 #> [6] -0.015142319  # 2. Predict hazard ratios (risk) for a new cohort new_cohort <- data.frame(x1 = c(0, 1.5, -1), x2 = c(0, 1, 1)) risk_scores <- predict(fit, newdata = new_cohort, type = "risk") print(risk_scores) #> [1] 1.0000000 0.9551026 0.9889093

Extract linear predictors for the original data lp_train <- predict(fit, type = "lp") head(lp_train) #> [1]  2.2995675 -1.3674625 -0.9769177 -3.3279029  1.2543794 -2.4888121  # 2. title: Predictions from a survMixBayes model — predict.survMixBayes
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(301) n <- 150 trt <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # Simulate Weibull AFT data true_time <- rweibull(n, shape = 1.5, scale = exp(1 + 0.8 * trt)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.1) true_obs_time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) true_status <- as.integer(true_time <= cens_time)  # Induce linkage mismatch errors in approximately 20% of records is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.2) obs_time <- true_obs_time obs_status <- true_status mismatch_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1)  shuffled <- sample(mismatch_idx) obs_time[mismatch_idx] <- obs_time[shuffled] obs_status[mismatch_idx] <- obs_status[shuffled]  linked_df <- data.frame(time = obs_time, status = obs_status, trt = trt) adj <- adjMixBayes(linked.data = linked_df)  fit <- plsurvreg(   survival::Surv(time, status) ~ trt,   dist = "weibull",   adjustment = adj,   control = list(     iterations = 200,     burnin.iterations = 100,     seed = 123   ) )  # Create a design matrix for new covariate values newdata <- stats::model.matrix(~ trt, data = data.frame(trt = c(0, 1)))  # Predict posterior mean linear predictors for each latent component preds <- predict(fit, newdata = newdata, se.fit = TRUE, interval = "credible") print(preds$component1) print(preds$component2) } # }

code: library(survival) set.seed(104) n <- 200  # 1. Simulate covariates age_centered <- rnorm(n, 0, 5) treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # 2. Simulate true survival times true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.05 * age_centered - 0.6 * treatment)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.2) time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) status <- as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time)  # 3. Induce 15% Exchangeable Linkage Error (ELE) mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.15 * n)) linked_age <- age_centered linked_trt <- treatment  # False links drawn uniformly from the target population false_link_idx <- sample(1:n, size = length(mis_idx), replace = TRUE) linked_age[mis_idx] <- age_centered[false_link_idx] linked_trt[mis_idx] <- treatment[false_link_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(time = time, status = status,                           age = linked_age, treatment = linked_trt)  # 4. Fit the adjusted Cox PH model adj <- adjELE(linked.data = linked_data, m.rate = 0.15) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + treatment, adjustment = adj)  # 5. Print the basic model output print(fit) #> Call: #> plcoxph(formula = Surv(time, status) ~ age + treatment, adjustment = adj) #>  #> Coefficients: #>       age  treatment   #>  -0.01234    0.11545   #>  #> n= 200, number of events= 41

code: library(survival) set.seed(104) n <- 200  # 1. Simulate covariates age_centered <- rnorm(n, 0, 5) treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # 2. Simulate true survival times true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.05 * age_centered - 0.6 * treatment)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.2) time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) status <- as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time)  # 3. Induce 15% Exchangeable Linkage Error (ELE) mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.15 * n)) linked_age <- age_centered linked_trt <- treatment  # False links drawn uniformly from the target population false_link_idx <- sample(1:n, size = length(mis_idx), replace = TRUE) linked_age[mis_idx] <- age_centered[false_link_idx] linked_trt[mis_idx] <- treatment[false_link_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(time = time, status = status,                           age = linked_age, treatment = linked_trt)  # 4. Fit the adjusted Cox PH model adj <- adjELE(linked.data = linked_data, m.rate = 0.15) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + treatment, adjustment = adj)  # 5. Generate and print the detailed statistical summary sum_fit <- summary(fit) print(sum_fit) #> Call: #> plcoxph(formula = Surv(time, status) ~ age + treatment, adjustment = adj) #>  #> n=200, number of events=41 #>  #>               coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|) #> age       -0.01234   0.98774  0.03724 -0.331    0.740 #> treatment  0.11545   1.12238  0.40019  0.289    0.773 #>  #> Confidence Intervals for Hazard Ratios: #>           exp(coef) lower 0.95 upper 0.95 #> age          0.9877     0.9182      1.063 #> treatment    1.1224     0.5123      2.459 #>

title: Summarizing Cox PH Mixture Fits — summary.coxphMixture
code: library(survival) set.seed(201)  # Simulate survival data (N = 200) n <- 200 x1 <- rnorm(n) x2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.5 * x1 - 0.5 * x2)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)  # Simulate auxiliary match scores and linkage errors # Lower match scores correspond to a higher probability of mismatch match_score <- runif(n, 0.5, 1.0) is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = 1 - match_score)  # Induce linkage errors by shuffling covariates of mismatched records linked_x1 <- x1 linked_x2 <- x2 mis_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1) shuffled_idx <- sample(mis_idx) linked_x1[mis_idx] <- x1[shuffled_idx] linked_x2[mis_idx] <- x2[shuffled_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(   time = pmin(true_time, cens_time),   status = as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time),   x1 = linked_x1, x2 = linked_x2,   match_score = match_score )  # Fit the Cox PH Mixture Model (Slawski et al., 2023) adj <- adjMixture(linked.data = linked_data, m.formula = ~ match_score) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ x1 + x2, adjustment = adj,                control = list(max.iter = 15))  # Print detailed statistical summary sum_fit <- summary(fit) print(sum_fit) #>  #> Call: #> plcoxph(formula = Surv(time, status) ~ x1 + x2, adjustment = adj,  #>     control = list(max.iter = 15)) #>  #> --- Outcome Model (Cox PH) --- #>       coef exp(coef) se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|)     #> x1  1.1366    3.1160   0.2375  4.785 1.71e-06 *** #> x2 -1.3127    0.2691   0.4236 -3.099  0.00194 **  #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> --- Hazard Ratios & Confidence Intervals --- #>    exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95 #> x1    3.1160     0.3209    1.9562    4.9635 #> x2    0.2691     3.7161    0.1173    0.6173 #>  #> --- Mismatch Indicator Model --- #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   #> (Intercept)   -5.687      2.337  -2.434   0.0149 * #> match_score    7.128      2.977   2.395   0.0166 * #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Average Estimated Correct Match Rate: 0.4527  #> Events: 113  / Total: 200  #> Iterations: 15  #>

title: Bayesian two-component mixture survival regression model — survregMixBayes
code:
if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Example: Bayesian mixture survival model fit to linked survival data # with induced linkage mismatch errors  # 1. Simulate a linked survival dataset set.seed(301) n <- 150 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), ncol = 2) colnames(X) <- c("x1", "x2")  # Generate survival times from a Weibull AFT model true_time <- rweibull(   n,   shape = 1.5,   scale = exp(0.5 * X[, 1] - 0.5 * X[, 2]) )  # Apply right-censoring cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.1) event <- as.integer(true_time <= cens_time) obs_time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time)  # Induce linkage mismatch errors in approximately 20% of records is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.2) mismatch_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1)  shuffled <- sample(mismatch_idx) obs_time[mismatch_idx] <- obs_time[shuffled] event[mismatch_idx] <- event[shuffled]  y <- cbind(time = obs_time, event = event)  # 2. Fit the Bayesian two-component mixture survival model # Note: Iterations are set artificially low for run time fit <- survregMixBayes(   X = X,   y = y,   dist = "weibull",   control = list(iterations = 200, burnin.iterations = 100, seed = 123) )  # 3. Inspect posterior summaries # (Label switching is handled automatically) cat("Component 1 (Correct Links):
") print(colMeans(fit$estimates$coefficients))  cat("Component 2 (Incorrect Links):
") print(colMeans(fit$estimates$m.coefficients))  cat("Estimated probability of correct linkage:
") print(mean(fit$estimates$theta)) } # }

title: Variance-Covariance Matrix for coxphELE Objects — vcov.coxphELE
code: library(survival) set.seed(104) n <- 200  # 1. Simulate covariates age_centered <- rnorm(n, 0, 5) treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # 2. Simulate true survival times true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.05 * age_centered - 0.6 * treatment)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.2) time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) status <- as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time)  # 3. Induce 15% Exchangeable Linkage Error (ELE) mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.15 * n)) linked_age <- age_centered linked_trt <- treatment  # False links drawn uniformly from the target population false_link_idx <- sample(1:n, size = length(mis_idx), replace = TRUE) linked_age[mis_idx] <- age_centered[false_link_idx] linked_trt[mis_idx] <- treatment[false_link_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(time = time, status = status,                           age = linked_age, treatment = linked_trt)  # 4. Fit the adjusted Cox PH model adj <- adjELE(linked.data = linked_data, m.rate = 0.15) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + treatment, adjustment = adj)  # 5. Extract the sandwich variance-covariance matrix vmat <- vcov(fit) print(vmat) #>                    age    treatment #> age        0.001386645 -0.004720918 #> treatment -0.004720918  0.160148790

title: Extract Variance-Covariance Matrix from a coxphMixture Object — vcov.coxphMixture
code: library(survival) set.seed(201)  # Simulate survival data (N = 200) n <- 200 x1 <- rnorm(n) x2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) true_time <- rexp(n, rate = exp(0.5 * x1 - 0.5 * x2)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.5)  # Simulate auxiliary match scores and linkage errors # Lower match scores correspond to a higher probability of mismatch match_score <- runif(n, 0.5, 1.0) is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, prob = 1 - match_score)  # Induce linkage errors by shuffling covariates of mismatched records linked_x1 <- x1 linked_x2 <- x2 mis_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1) shuffled_idx <- sample(mis_idx) linked_x1[mis_idx] <- x1[shuffled_idx] linked_x2[mis_idx] <- x2[shuffled_idx]  linked_data <- data.frame(   time = pmin(true_time, cens_time),   status = as.numeric(true_time <= cens_time),   x1 = linked_x1, x2 = linked_x2,   match_score = match_score )  # Fit the Cox PH Mixture Model (Slawski et al., 2023) adj <- adjMixture(linked.data = linked_data, m.formula = ~ match_score) fit <- plcoxph(Surv(time, status) ~ x1 + x2, adjustment = adj,                control = list(max.iter = 15))  # Extract the variance-covariance matrix # Note: For outcome coefficients (beta) and mismatch coefficients (gamma) vmat <- vcov(fit) print(vmat) #>                        x1          x2 m.(Intercept) m.match_score #> x1             0.05642079 -0.04016553     0.1909437    -0.2567206 #> x2            -0.04016553  0.17945502    -0.2074337     0.2891386 #> m.(Intercept)  0.19094373 -0.20743369     5.4601921    -6.8925090 #> m.match_score -0.25672058  0.28913860    -6.8925090     8.8613058

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(125) n <- 300  # 1. Simulate true categorical data with dependency exposure <- sample(c("low", "high"), n, replace = TRUE)  # Induce dependency - High exposure -> higher disease probability prob_disease <- ifelse(exposure == "high", 0.7, 0.3) true_disease <- ifelse(runif(n) < prob_disease, "yes", "no")  # 2. Induce 15% linkage error mis_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.15 * n)) obs_disease <- true_disease obs_disease[mis_idx] <- sample(obs_disease[mis_idx])  linked_df <- data.frame(exposure = exposure, disease = obs_disease)  # 3. Fit the adjusted contingency table model adj <- adjMixture(linked.data = linked_df, m.rate = 0.15) fit <- plctable(~ exposure + disease, adjustment = adj)  # 4. Extract the variance-covariance matrix of the cell probabilities vmat <- vcov(fit) print(vmat) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(301) n <- 150 trt <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)  # Simulate Weibull AFT data true_time <- rweibull(n, shape = 1.5, scale = exp(1 + 0.8 * trt)) cens_time <- rexp(n, rate = 0.1) true_obs_time <- pmin(true_time, cens_time) true_status <- as.integer(true_time <= cens_time)  # Induce linkage mismatch errors in approximately 20% of records is_mismatch <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.2) obs_time <- true_obs_time obs_status <- true_status mismatch_idx <- which(is_mismatch == 1)  shuffled <- sample(mismatch_idx) obs_time[mismatch_idx] <- obs_time[shuffled] obs_status[mismatch_idx] <- obs_status[shuffled]  linked_df <- data.frame(time = obs_time, status = obs_status, trt = trt)  adj <- adjMixBayes(linked.data = linked_df)  fit <- plsurvreg(   survival::Surv(time, status) ~ trt,   dist = "weibull",   adjustment = adj,   control = list(iterations = 200, burnin.iterations = 100, seed = 123) )  # Extract the empirical posterior covariance matrix for component 1 vcov_mat <- vcov(fit) print(vcov_mat) } # }
